title: File Permissions & ACLs
description: Linux file permissions are the foundation of system security. Every Production SRE and infrastructure engineer must understand the permission model cold — including SUID/SGID, sticky bits, ACLs, and how they interact. Permission misconfiguration is a leading cause of security vulnerabilities in production.

sections.title: The Permission Triplet: Owner-Group-Other
sections.description: Every file and directory has three permission sets: owner (u), group (g), and others (o). Each set has three bits: read (r=4), write (w=2), execute (x=1). This 9-bit mode (plus 3 special bits) is the standard Unix permission model.
sections.codeBlock:
# Permission modes: rwxr-xr--
#                  uuu ggg ooo
#                  user group others

ls -l /etc/passwd
# -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 2.8K Mar 15 12:34 /etc/passwd
# ^  |  |  |     ^    ^     ^
# |  u  g  o     |    |     size
# type           owner group

# Numeric (octal) representation:
# rwx = 4+2+1 = 7
# rw- = 4+2+0 = 6
# r-x = 4+0+1 = 5
# r-- = 4+0+0 = 4
# --- = 0+0+0 = 0

chmod 755 script.sh    # rwxr-xr-x — owner: all, group/other: read+execute
chmod 644 config.conf  # rw-r--r-- — owner: read+write, group/other: read
chmod 600 id_rsa       # rw------- — SSH private key: owner only!
chmod 700 ~/.ssh       # rwx------ — directory: owner only (lock down SSH)

chmod u+x script.sh    # Add execute for user
chmod g-w file.txt     # Remove write for group
chmod o=r file.txt     # Set others to read-only
chmod a+rx /app/bin    # Add read+execute for all (user,group,other)

sections.title: Directories: Execute Bit = Traversal
sections.description: On directories, the permission bits have different meanings: r = list files, w = create/delete files, x = traverse/search (enter) the directory. Without the execute bit, you can't access any files in the directory even if you know their names — a common source of confusion.
sections.codeBlock:
# Directory permissions explained:
# r (read):    can list directory contents (ls)
# w (write):   can create, rename, delete files in the directory
# x (execute): can traverse the directory (cd into it, access files by name)

# Common directory permissions:
chmod 755 /home/user      # rwxr-xr-x — standard: owner writes, others traverse
chmod 700 /home/user/.ssh # rwx------ — SSH directory: only owner!
chmod 1777 /tmp           # rwxrwxrwt — world-writable with sticky bit

# Gotcha: read WITHOUT execute on a directory
chmod 644 /restricted-dir # rw-r--r--
ls /restricted-dir        # Can LIST files (has r)
cat /restricted-dir/file  # Permission denied! Can't traverse (no x)

# Minimum permissions for web server to serve files:
# Directory: r-x (5) — traverse and list
# File:      r-- (4) — read only
# No need for w (2) on files or directories

sections.title: Special Permissions: SUID, SGID, Sticky Bit
sections.description: Three special permission bits add additional behavior. SUID (4000) — run a file with the owner's privileges (e.g., passwd runs as root). SGID (2000) — on files, run with group's privileges; on directories, new files inherit the directory's group. Sticky Bit (1000) — only file owners can delete their files (used on /tmp). These are frequent interview topics.
sections.codeBlock:
# SUID (Set User ID) — runs as file owner, not the user executing it
chmod u+s /usr/bin/passwd  # Set SUID (s in user execute position)
ls -l /usr/bin/passwd
# -rwsr-xr-x ... passwd — the 's' means SUID + executable

# Check all SUID binaries on your system:
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null

# SGID (Set Group ID) — on directories: new files inherit group
chmod g+s /shared/project   # Set SGID (s in group execute position)
# Now any file created in /shared/project belongs to the directory's group

# Sticky Bit — only owners can delete their own files
chmod +t /tmp               # Set sticky bit (t in others execute position)
ls -ld /tmp
# drwxrwxrwt ... /tmp — the 't' means sticky bit is set

# === Interview: SUID Security ===
# Why is SUID dangerous? If a SUID root binary has a vulnerability,
# any user can escalate to root.
# Mitigation: minimize SUID binaries (use sudo instead),
# mount filesystems with 'nosuid' option where possible.

# Numeric shortcuts:
chmod 4755 script  # SUID + rwxr-xr-x
chmod 2755 dir     # SGID + rwxr-xr-x
chmod 1777 /tmp    # Sticky bit + rwxrwxrwx

sections.title: Access Control Lists (ACLs)
sections.description: POSIX ACLs extend the traditional permission model to support multiple users and groups. When the basic u/g/o model is insufficient, ACLs provide fine-grained control. Production use case: shared team directories where multiple service accounts and humans need different access levels.
sections.codeBlock:
# Check if ACLs are enabled on a filesystem
mount | grep acl
# (Most modern filesystems have ACLs enabled by default)

# View ACLs on a file/directory
getfacl /shared/project
# file: shared/project
# owner: alice
# group: dev-team
# user::rwx
# user:bob:r-x       # Bob has read+execute only
# group::r-x
# mask::r-x
# other::---

# Set ACLs
setfacl -m u:bob:rx /shared/project    # Add Bob with read+execute
setfacl -m g:devops:rwx /shared/tools  # Add devops group with full access
setfacl -m o::--- /shared/project      # Remove all access for others

# Recursive ACL (be careful — performance impact)
setfacl -R -m u:ci-user:rx /app/deploy

# Remove specific ACL entry
setfacl -x u:bob /shared/project

# Default ACL — new files/dirs inherit these ACLs
setfacl -d -m g:dev-team:rwx /shared/project

# === Enterprise Insight ===
# ACLs add complexity. Before extending ACLs, ask:
# Can I solve this with Unix groups in /etc/group?
# ACLs are harder to audit and debug than traditional permissions.
# In Enterprise environments, we minimize ACL usage and rely on:
# - Clear group membership
# - Service accounts with minimal permissions
# - Config management (Ansible/Terraform) to enforce permissions

sections.title: umask & Default Permissions
sections.description: The umask determines default permissions for newly created files and directories. It's a mask (inverted) — the permissions are base - umask. Files default to 666 (rw-rw-rw-), directories to 777 (rwxrwxrwx). The umask subtracts from these.
sections.codeBlock:
# Current umask value
umask                     # 0022 (most common)
umask -S                 # Symbolic: u=rwx,g=rx,o=rx

# How umask works:
# File defaults:   666 (rw-rw-rw-)
# Minus umask:     022 (----w--w-)
# Result:          644 (rw-r--r--)

# Directory defaults: 777 (rwxrwxrwx)
# Minus umask:        022 (----w--w-)
# Result:             755 (rwxr-xr-x)

# Common umask values:
# 0022 — rw-r--r-- files, rwxr-xr-x dirs (standard)
# 0027 — rw-r-----, rwxr-x--- (shared team with restricted group)
# 0077 — rw-------, rwx------ (private — SSH keys!)

umask 0077               # Set for this shell session
# Add to ~/.bashrc for persistence

# === Interview ===
# Q: 'A user reports that files they create in /shared/project
#     are not accessible by their teammates. What's wrong?'
# A: Check umask. If it's 0077, files are created with no group access.
#    Fix: set umask 0027 in the team's .bashrc or the shared script.

sections.title: Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'What does chmod 4755 do?'

A: '4 indicates the SUID bit is set. 755 means rwxr-xr-x. So the file runs with the owner's privileges regardless of who executes it. Common for utilities like passwd and ping that need elevated access.'

Q: 'How do you audit permissions across a fleet of 1000 Linux servers?'

A: 'At Enterprise scale, we never SSH into individual servers to check permissions. Instead, configuration management (Ansible, Chef, Puppet) enforces desired permissions via declarative config. We also use auditing tools like auditd with watches on sensitive paths, and drift detection that compares current permissions against a golden baseline. For security-critical checks (SUID, world-writable files), we run automated scans and alert on changes.'

Q: 'Scenario: You need to give a CI/CD service account write access to /app/deploy but read-only access to /app/config. The owner is deploy-user, group is dev-team. How?'

A: 'Traditional permissions: if ci-user needs different access than deploy-user, the basic u/g/o model won't work. I'd use ACLs: setfacl -m u:ci-user:rwx /app/deploy and setfacl -m u:ci-user:rx /app/config. Or create a dedicated group (ci-agents), add ci-user to it, and set group permissions accordingly. The group approach is simpler to audit.'

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• man chmod — Change file modes bits
  • man getfacl / setfacl — POSIX ACL management
  • visudo — Edit sudoers safely

learningObjectives: Understand special Permissions: SUID, SGID, Sticky Bit

analogy.title: Permissions as House Keys
analogy.description: Each file is a room. The owner has the master key (read/write/execute). Group members have a shared key (read/execute). Others might only peek through the window (read). The sticky bit on /tmp is like a coat check - you can hang your coat, but only you can take it back.

commonMistakes.mistake: Ignoring log rotation until disk fills up
commonMistakes.solution: Configure logrotate before deploying; monitor /var/log usage

commonMistakes.mistake: Disabling SELinux/AppArmor instead of fixing policies
commonMistakes.solution: Use `audit2allow` to generate policies from denials; keep MAC enabled
Add `USER nonroot` in Dockerfile; use `--user` flag at runtime

commonMistakes.mistake: Not setting resource limits on containers
commonMistakes.solution: Always set `--cpus` and `--memory`; configure cgroup limits in Kubernetes

practiceLabs.title: Explore: The Permission Triplet: Owner-Group-Other
practiceLabs.task: Run `ls` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man ls` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Directories: Execute Bit = Traversal
practiceLabs.task: Run `chmod` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man chmod` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Special Permissions: SUID, SGID, Sticky Bit
practiceLabs.task: Run `cat` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man cat` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

quickReference.title: Quick Reference
quickReference.content:
| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `ls` | The Permission Triplet: Owner-Group-Other |
| `chmod` | The Permission Triplet: Owner-Group-Other |
| `chmod` | Directories: Execute Bit = Traversal |
| `ls` | Directories: Execute Bit = Traversal |
| `cat` | Directories: Execute Bit = Traversal |
| `chmod` | Special Permissions: SUID, SGID, Sticky Bit |
| `ls` | Special Permissions: SUID, SGID, Sticky Bit |
| `find` | Special Permissions: SUID, SGID, Sticky Bit |
| `mount` | Access Control Lists (ACLs) |
| `getfacl` | Access Control Lists (ACLs) |
| `setfacl` | Access Control Lists (ACLs) |
| `umask` | umask & Default Permissions |

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect arguments, wrong working directory, or stale cached data
troubleshooting.solution: Verify arguments with `man ls`; check current path with `pwd`; clear cache if applicable

troubleshooting.cause: Service not reloaded or syntax error in config file
troubleshooting.solution: Validate config syntax with built-in test flag; reload service with `systemctl reload` or `restart`

troubleshooting.symptom: permissions operation slow or timing out
troubleshooting.cause: Resource exhaustion (disk I/O, memory pressure, or lock contention)
troubleshooting.solution: Check `iostat`, `free -m`, and `top`; look for stuck processes with `ps aux --sort=-%cpu`

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect file ownership or overly restrictive permissions
troubleshooting.solution: Inspect with `ls -la`; fix with `chown`/ `chmod`; check ACLs with `getfacl`

quiz.question: What is The Permission Triplet: Owner-Group-Other?
options: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
A tool for managing disk partitions
quiz.answer: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
quiz.explanation: The The Permission Triplet: Owner-Group-Other is the foundation of the Linux filesystem — everything in the system is organized under this structure.

quiz.question: What does `ls` do?
quiz.answer: Lists files and directories in the current or specified path
quiz.explanation: Use `ls` to explore directory contents. Combine with flags like `-l` (long format), `-a` (hidden files), and `-h` (human-readable sizes).

quiz.question: Which command would you use to view disk usage of the permissions directory?
quiz.explanation: `du -sh` (disk usage, summary, human-readable) shows the total size of a directory and its contents.

quiz.question: A user reports "Permission denied" when trying to access a permissions directory. What is the first command you should run?
quiz.explanation: `ls -ld` shows the directory's permissions and ownership. Always inspect before making changes — starting with `chmod 777` is a security anti-pattern.

quiz.question: In a permissions scenario, your monitoring alerts show ls failing intermittently. What is the correct debugging workflow?
options: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
Ignore the alert and wait for it to resolve
quiz.answer: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
quiz.explanation: A structured debugging workflow prevents cascading failures. Always diagnose before acting, especially in production environments where downtime has direct business impact.

interviewTips: FAANG Architect's Note:  Permission management at Internet scale is fundamentally different from single-server Linux
bestPractices: Use version control for all configuration (/etc, scripts, Dockerfiles)
Implement infrastructure as code; avoid manual server changes
Test changes in staging before production; have rollback plans
enterprisePerspective: In enterprise environments, file permissions & acls is managed through automation (Ansible, Terraform), monitored centrally (Prometheus, Datadog), and governed by compliance policies (CIS benchmarks, SOC2). Changes go through change advisory boards with rollback procedures documented.
